Digital Marketing Campaign Performance Analysis

Uncovering What Drives ROI, Identifying Wasted Spend, and Timing the Perfect Q4 Campaign Push

Business Objective¶

A digital marketing team running campaigns across four channels — Social, Search, Email, and Display — needed answers to three critical questions their dashboard wasn't telling them:

  • Which channels are actually generating returns, and which are consuming budget without proportional results?
  • Which audience segments convert best, and are we spending enough to reach them?
  • Is our Q4 revenue spike something we created with more spend — or organic seasonal demand we could be capitalizing on more?

Without answers, the team risks over-investing in underperforming channels, under-investing in high-efficiency ones, and missing the optimal window to push Q4 budget for maximum return.

What This Analysis Delivers¶

This analysis applies a full three-layer analytical framework across 12 months of campaign data, 4 channels, and 3 audience segments to produce data-backed budget allocation and campaign timing recommendations.

What → Why → So What

  • Descriptive — maps the performance landscape across channels, segments, and months
  • Diagnostic — identifies the root causes behind ROI gaps, seasonal patterns, and segment inefficiencies
  • Prescriptive — delivers specific channel budget recommendations and an optimized Q4 campaign brief

Dataset Overview¶

  • 300 campaigns · 12 months · 4 channels · 3 audience segments
  • 12 variables including spend, revenue, impressions, clicks, conversions, CTR, conversion rate, and ROI

Tools Used¶

Python · Pandas · NumPy · Matplotlib · Seaborn · Jupyter Notebook

Analyst¶

Dataset Variables¶

campaign_id:unique identifier

channel:Social / Search / Email / Display

audience_segment:Young Adults / Professionals / Seniors

month:Jan–Dec 2023

impressions:total ad views

clicks:total link clicks

conversions:completed purchases

spend:USD campaign cost

revenue:USD revenue generated

ctr:click-through rate %

conversion_rate:conversions / clicks %

roi:(revenue - spend) / spend

Descriptive — what happened?

1. What is the overall distribution of ROI, spend, and revenue across all campaigns?

Data Shows that across 300 campaign, average roi was $27 and median roi was $13.¶

This means high performing ROI campaign pull the average up.¶

There are about 110 campaign that has an roi around $10. About 70 campaign has ROI of around $13. About 30 campaign has an RoI of around $25. About 30 campaign has ROI of around $35.¶

This means that few campaigns have high roi , and many campaigns have low roi¶

Data show that across 300 campaign, Average Spending was $8,451 and Median spending was $7,735.This means there are campaigns that has higher spending.¶

Data shows that about 137 campaigns (46%) was in the range of having spending spending around $5000 to $9000.¶

About 26 campaigns (8.6%) have spending exceeding $15000.¶

This means there are some campaign that have very high spending.And most campaigns have been around average spending.¶

Data shows that about 216 campaigns has revenue less than $250,000 . About 24 campaigns has revenue exceeding 1 million dollars . About 2 campaigns has revenue exceeding 5 million dollars.¶

This means many campaign belongs to lower revenue and less campaigns belong to high revenue.¶

2. Which channel generates the highest average ROI and revenue?

Search channel had the highest ROI rate of around 55%, the gap is wide followed by Email channel around (32%) , Social Channel(25%) then Display channel around (5%) at the bottom of lowest ROI rate.¶

This means Search Channel is the highest Roi genearator thus it performs best, then the Email and Social Channel is only around half of what Search roi can generate.While Display Channel performs very poor across the poor¶

Data shows a wide gap of Search Channel having avg revenue around $930k , followed by Social around $267k, then Email channel at around $172k and Display at around$46k average.¶

This means Search performs outstanding in terms of averge revenue, display Channel performs very poor, then Email and Social barely generates average revenue to have fair performance.¶

Monthly spend showed a steady upward trend throughout 2023, increasing from $186.9K in January to $302.2K in December, with the highest investment levels concentrated in Q4.¶

Revenue performance fluctuated across the first three quarters, reaching its lowest point in August ($1.96M) following a mid-year slowdown. However, revenue accelerated significantly in Q4, ultimately peaking in December at $23.13M.¶

The most significant insight is that Q4 generated the strongest overall efficiency and returns. From October through December, revenue growth substantially outpaced spend growth, indicating highly effective scaling strategies and strong seasonal demand during the year-end period.¶

Diagnostic : Why did that happen?

Why does the top channel outperform — is it CTR, conversion rate, or spend?

=== CTR rank vs Conversion Rate rank vs ROI rank ===
         CTR_rank  CVR_rank  ROI_rank  Spend_rank
CHANNEL                                          
Display         4         4         4           3
Email           3         1         2           4
Search          1         2         1           1
Social          2         3         3           2
Correlation Strength
0.00–0.19 Very weak
0.20–0.39 Weak
0.40–0.59 Moderate
0.60–0.79 Strong
0.80–1.00 Very strong

Core finding¶

Search dominates because it wins at both ends of the funnel — it attracts the most clicks (CTR rank 1) and converts them at near-top efficiency (conversion rate rank 2), resulting in the highest ROI (rank 1) in the portfolio. Search's dominance is structural, not accidental.

The most actionable finding — Email's hidden efficiency¶

Email ranks last in spend (rank 4) but first in conversion rate (rank 1) and second in ROI (rank 2). This means the highest-converting channel in the portfolio is receiving the smallest budget. Every dollar shifted from Display to Email is likely to generate a better return — Email converts clicks to purchases more reliably than any other channel but is being systematically underfunded.

The drain — Display's budget problem¶

Display ranks third in spend but last in CTR, conversion rate, and ROI simultaneously. It is the only channel where spend rank is materially better than performance rank — meaning the business is paying more for Display than its results justify.

What ROI actually depends on¶

The strongest correlation with ROI across all campaigns is revenue (r = 0.92), confirming that high-ROI campaigns are genuinely high-revenue campaigns — not low-cost illusions. ROI gains are real and scale with actual business outcomes.

This means...¶

Search earns its budget through funnel-wide dominance, but Email is the portfolio's most underfunded asset — it converts better than any channel yet receives the least investment, making it the single highest-priority reallocation opportunity in the dataset.

The correlation heatmap shows that both ROI and revenue have moderate to very strong positive correlations with all other variables, meaning they tend to increase alongside marketing activity and performance metrics such as spend, clicks, and conversions, rather than move independently.¶

Higher CTR is associated with higher ROI in some channels (especially Search), but CTR alone is not a reliable predictor of ROI across all channels, meaning other factors (like conversion quality, cost, and targeting) are likely driving ROI more strongly.¶

Scatter plot shows that for Search Channel,higher conversion rate drives higher ROI, but for other channels it doesnt show any relationship¶

Some Search Channel campagign scales best that for higher spending theres higher revenue.¶

        Nov         18.43           40.00
        Dec          3.44           51.99

Insight¶

  1. Q4 growth was demand-driven, not just spend-driven. Revenue grew much faster than spend — especially in September (+295% revenue vs +34% spend) and December (+52% revenue vs +3% spend).

  2. ROI efficiency peaked in Q4. Revenue per $1 spent jumped from 12.4x in August to 44.1x in October, 52.1x in November, and 76.5x in December, showing much stronger return efficiency.

  3. Audience engagement increased significantly in Q4. CTR rose from 3.8% (Non-Q4) to 5.8% (Q4), indicating users were more responsive to campaigns during the holiday season.

  4. Conversion rates stayed stable despite revenue surging. Conversion rate remained around 3.7–4.0%, meaning higher revenue came from increased high-intent traffic rather than weaker conversion quality.

  5. Seasonality was the key driver behind October–December peaks. Holiday shopping behavior, promotional periods, and stronger consumer intent amplified campaign performance, while marketing spend efficiently captured that demand.

3. Which channel audience segment combination is being under-invested in?

CHANNEL       SEGMENT  avg_roi  avg_spend  roi_rank  spend_rank  opportunity_gap
  Email Professionals    63.33    5752.79         2           9                7
  Email  Young Adults    18.60    4123.15         6          11                5

--- Positive opportunity_gap = high ROI but low spend = underinvested ---

Heatmap verdict¶

Comparing ROI and spend heatmaps side by side reveals a clear pattern — the highest-returning combinations are not receiving proportional investment, while the lowest-returning combinations continue to consume meaningful budget.

Full ROI matrix by channel × segment¶

Channel Professionals Young Adults Seniors
Search 119.8 ✦ 29.8 13.8
Email 63.3 ↑ 18.4 9.2
Social 24.1 18.6 7.4
Display 8.5 1.3 0.25

✦ Best performing combination — well funded, protect this budget ↑ Highest opportunity gap — high ROI, systematically underfunded

The three strategic findings¶

1. Email × Professionals — the portfolio's biggest missed opportunity ROI of 63.3 with below-average spend makes this the most under-invested combination in the dataset. Email already leads all channels in conversion rate (G1 finding) and Professionals are the cheapest segment to convert at $20 per conversion (D3 finding). Together they compound into the highest-efficiency pairing that is being systematically ignored.

2. Search × Professionals — the core asset ROI of 119.8 is the highest single combination in the entire matrix and it is already well-funded. This combination should be protected at all costs — it is the revenue engine of the portfolio. Any budget cuts should come from elsewhere.

3. Display — a universal underperformer Display produces ROI between 0.25 and 8.5 across all three segments. There is no combination where Display justifies its spend. It is the only channel that fails across every audience simultaneously, making it the clearest reallocation target in the portfolio.

Segment efficiency summary¶

  • Professionals respond strongly across Search and Email — high intent, high conversion, lowest cost per conversion
  • Young Adults respond best to Search and Social — engaged at the click level but require the right channel
  • Seniors show weak ROI across all channels (≤13.8) — current targeting and messaging is not resonating with this segment

This means...¶

The business is over-investing in Display across all segments and under-investing in Email × Professionals — the combination with the second-highest ROI in the portfolio. Reallocating even a portion of Display budget to Email campaigns targeting Professionals represents the highest-confidence budget improvement available in this dataset.

Prescriptive : What to recommend?

1. Which channels should receive more budget, which should be cut, and which need fixing?

P1 — Channel Budget Recommendations¶

Verdict: Reallocate from Display into Email and Search (Professionals)

---

Search — Maintain, Selective Scaling¶

Search is the portfolio's strongest channel (ROI rank 1) and should be protected. Scale only within the Professionals segment where ROI reaches 119.85 on $17,823 avg spend — the highest single combination in the dataset.

Fix: Search × Seniors returns only 13.81 ROI despite $11,034 avg spend (-3 opportunity gap). Rework targeting and creative before adding further budget to this segment.

Email — Increase Budget (Highest Priority)¶

Email is the most underfunded channel in the portfolio — conversion rate rank 1, spend rank 4. Two combinations justify immediate scaling:

  • Email × Professionals: ROI 63.33 on only $5,753 avg spend (+7 gap)
  • Email × Young Adults: ROI 18.60 on $4,123 avg spend (+5 gap)

Every dollar shifted into Email × Professionals is currently generating the second-highest ROI in the entire channel-segment matrix at the lowest cost to execute.

Social — Maintain, Optimize Seniors¶

Social performs adequately for Professionals (ROI 49.65) and Young Adults (ROI 18.90). Hold current budget levels for these segments. Shift spend away from Social × Seniors (ROI 8.70 on $7,173 spend, -2 opportunity gap) toward stronger combinations.

Display — Cut Aggressively¶

Display fails across every segment with no exception:

Segment ROI Avg Spend Opportunity Gap
Professionals 8.50 $8,471 -4
Young Adults 1.31 $6,402 —
Seniors 0.25 $5,688 —

Reallocate Display budget into Email × Professionals and Email × Young Adults for immediate ROI improvement.

Recommended Budget Direction

Channel Action Priority
Search Maintain + fix Seniors High
Email Increase Highest
Social Maintain + fix Seniors Medium
Display Cut aggressively Immediate

P2 — Q4 Campaign Push Recommendation¶

Verdict: Launch in December · Channel: Email · Audience: Professionals

---

When — December¶

December delivers the highest campaign efficiency in the dataset:

  • Revenue-to-spend ratio: 76.53x (vs 37.36x in January)
  • Total revenue: $23.13M on only $302K spend
  • Revenue growth still accelerating at +51.99% from November

Q4 overall confirms the seasonal advantage:

Metric Q4 Non-Q4
Avg ROI 41.57 22.30
Avg CTR 5.75% 3.82%

Timing note: G2 showed September revenue grew +295% on only +34% spend growth, meaning demand begins building before Q4 officially starts. Launch campaigns in late September to capture early demand before competitors enter the window.

Which Channel — Email¶

Email is the highest-incremental-return channel available for scaling:

  • Email × Professionals ROI: 63.33 on only $5,752 avg spend
  • Largest positive opportunity gap in the dataset: +7
  • Search × Professionals ROI is higher (119.85) but already heavily funded at $17,823 avg spend with zero opportunity gap

Email offers the strongest return on additional dollars invested. Search is already optimized — Email is not.

Which Audience — Professionals¶

Professionals produce the highest ROI across every channel:

Channel Professionals ROI Young Adults ROI Seniors ROI
Search 119.85 29.80 13.81
Email 63.33 18.60 9.20
Social 49.65 18.90 8.70

Combined with D3's finding that Professionals cost only $20 per conversion vs Seniors at $42, they are both the highest-returning and most cost-efficient audience in the portfolio.

Final Recommendation

Launch Q4 push in late September, scale Email as the primary channel, and concentrate targeting on Professionals.

This combination captures early seasonal demand (G2), maximizes the most underfunded high-return channel (G1, G3), and targets the audience with the highest ROI and lowest acquisition cost (D3, G3) — backed by three independent diagnostic findings pointing to the same answer.
